keyLegislation: Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Act 2006 (AML/CTF Act)
keyConsiderations: Bitcoin must be held in accordance with the fund's investment strategy as required by SIS Regulation 4.09
The sole purpose test under s62 SIS Act must be satisfied — all investments must be maintained for the sole purpose of providing retirement benefits to members
SMSFs cannot acquire assets from related parties unless they are listed securities or business real property — Bitcoin purchased from a member's personal holdings is prohibited
The fund's trust deed must not contain provisions that restrict or prohibit cryptocurrency investments
Investment strategy must be updated to reflect the inclusion of digital assets, including risk assessment and liquidity considerations
Arm's length dealing requirements under s109 SIS Act apply — Bitcoin must be acquired at market value from unrelated parties
Separation of assets — Bitcoin must be held separately from members' personal assets and clearly identifiable as fund assets
SMSF trustees have a fiduciary duty to act in the best interests of all members and must be able to demonstrate the investment rationale
ATO has confirmed that cryptocurrency is a CGT asset for SMSFs and gains or losses are calculated under standard CGT rules
The 1/3 discount on capital gains applies to assets held longer than 12 months within the fund (taxed at 10% instead of 15%)
Segregated current pension assets may be entitled to a 0% tax rate on capital gains
Annual audit requirements mean the SMSF auditor must be able to verify the existence, ownership, and valuation of Bitcoin holdings
commonRisks: Breach of the sole purpose test if Bitcoin is used for personal purposes or speculative trading inconsistent with a retirement objective
Failure to update the investment strategy, which may result in a contravention notice from the ATO
Loss of private keys resulting in permanent loss of fund assets — trustees may be personally liable for negligence
Using a non-compliant or unregulated exchange that could expose the fund to counterparty risk
Inadvertent related-party transaction breaches if Bitcoin is transferred from a member's personal wallet
Concentration risk — over-allocation to a single volatile asset class without adequate diversification
Audit complications if the auditor cannot independently verify Bitcoin holdings or valuations
Non-compliance with AML/CTF requirements at the exchange level affecting the fund's ability to transact
Tax reporting errors due to the complexity of tracking cost bases across multiple transactions
Regulatory change risk — potential future restrictions on SMSFs holding digital assets
recommendedProfessionals: SMSF Specialist Adviser (SSA) with digital asset experience
SMSF Auditor (approved by ASIC under s128B SIS Act)
Registered Tax Agent with cryptocurrency expertise
Solicitor experienced in SMSF trust deed amendments
Financial Adviser holding an AFSL with authority to advise on SMSFs
